description: A child may 'kiwate' an apple, and a dog may bite a toy and let go. 'kiwate' ends at the teeth's grip or mark; 'kati' (cut) says material is divided, while 'nuola' (eat) says food is taken in.
articulatory_notes: Raise the back of the tongue to the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front 'i'. Round the lips for voiced 'w', then open into stressed central 'a'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for dental 't', release it, and finish with middle-height front 'e'.
sound_symbolism: Velar 'k' and dental 't' place two clean stops around broad stressed 'wa'. The mouth opens between contacts and closes farther forward, a compact physical mnemonic for teeth meeting something.
usage_notes: The bitten person or thing stands before 'kiwate', and its event noun is a bite. The word reports tooth contact; any consequence needs its own clause.

semantic_domains.activity: teeth closing on or into a person or thing
semantic_domains.physical: tooth contact strong enough to grip or mark
